title: Venice     IMG_2688
tags: venice vendig chiesadisanzaccaria giovannibellini madonnaandchildwithfoursaints
description:
Giovanni Bellini’s Madonna and Child with Four Saints located in the Chiesa di San Zaccaria.  This was one of his last paintings.
The work is set in a large niche, depicting a sacred conversation within an established scheme.
The sacra conversazione is a genre of religious devotional works that shows the Virgin Mary with the Christ child upon a throne, surrounded by saints and angels which united them all into one space of harmony.

title: Venice     IMG_2687
tags: venice vendig chiesadisanzaccaria
description: Chiesa di San Zaccaria, The Church of San Zaccaria, in Venice, contains many sights including a crypt and several art treasures including Giovanni Bellini’s Madonna and Child with Four Saints.
comments: Caffe_Paradiso: One of my favourite churches in Venice,. I was right by my hotel for a few years. I enjoyed visiting it. | thomas alan: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07] What was the hotel name? | Caffe_Paradiso: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/9021833@N06] Palazzo Pruli. I see from the website that the lounge has been seriously upgraded since I stayed there in 2014 and 2015. It had a large buffet breakfast which helped me get through most of the day. www.hotelpriuli.com/ This is the view from the lounge. There is a lot of activity along this fondamenta in the early morning as Venetians go to work or take their children to the school just across the canal. www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07/52151338295/in/galler... | Caffe_Paradiso: This is the view in the other direction one morning while I was enjoying breakfast at the window. The fellow in the first three pics eyed something floating in the canal and managed to scoop it up. www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07/albums/72157719205919... This is the album of the school yard where I was able to sneak in when the door was left open one morning. I had wanted to get in there to see the small out of sight windows in the sacristy of San Zaccaria. They provide light on the small altar at the back of the sacristy the way Bernini would have done in a couple of churches in Rome. Luckily no one was around to kick me out. As I was leaving there was a small parade of children forming. I took some pics as they left the yard as you see. It was quite fun. www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07/albums/72157719593437... | thomas alan: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07] A beautiful view | thomas alan: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07] It definitely looks like a great place to watch activity! | Caffe_Paradiso: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/9021833@N06] It was. Unfortunately the newly up-scaled lounge no longer appears to have small tables by the windows which overlook the fondamenta. I was lucky to be there when the place was a little more informal. it's a bit too glitzy now for my tastes. I was just thinking one has to enjoy what was has at the time. After staying at the Priuli a couple of years I found a little gem in Dorsoduro off Campo San Barnaba. I was a great little place run by a family that had owned the building for several hundred years. The little walled garden was a perfect place for an early breakfast or afternoon tea where gondoliers could be seen passing through the windows onto the canal behind; And I had a Very large single room on the ground floor. The place seems to have changed management and only a couple of rooms seem to be offered for guests now. It's sad. This the album of photos I took of the Locanda San Barnaba. www.flickr.com/photos/136891509@N07/albums/72157667566692...

description:
Venice, Italy.

"The Church of San Zaccaria is a 15th-century former monastic church in central Venice, Italy. It is a large edifice, located in the Campo San Zaccaria, just off the waterfront to the southeast of Piazza San Marco and St Mark's Basilica. It is dedicated to St. Zechariah, the father of John the Baptist.

The first church on the site was founded by Doge Giustiniano Participazio in the early 9th century to house the body of the saint to which it is dedicated, a gift of the Byzantine Emperor Leo V the Armenian, which it contains under the second altar on the right. The remains of various doges are buried in the crypt of the church. The original church was rebuilt in the 1170s (when the present campanile was built) and was replaced by a Gothic church in the 15th century. The remains of this building still stand, as the present church was built beside and not over it."

The old church is visible on the right.

comments: encantadissima: My previous: | Claudia DG: Tante volte mi sono chiesta se quando scatti segui i pensieri con cui accompagni le foto .. o se scatti e poi, solo poi, emergono i pensieri e le associazioni. In ogni caso sempre un momento di riflessione per noi. Grazie Livia 🎈 | tanitzergh: Cara. Che meraviglia di omaggio. | Hervé S, France: Bel omaggio ! Ho imparato l'italiano "con" Faber e gli altri gandi Italiani! ;-)))) Saluti da Bordeaux. | toscano libero: Mi gusto la canzone e la foto malinconica e bellissima | Gio guarda le stelle: bello.... tutto..... deliziosa la domanda di Claudia DG ... aspetto con curiosità la tua risposta.... | encantadissima: [https://www.flickr.com/photos/cladg] Gran bella domanda... segno che osservi le foto e segui con arguzia. Rispondo con piacere... Solitamente i miei scatti non hanno una finalità preordinata, ma non è una verità assoluta. A volte, poche, ho scattato cercando la foto allo scopo di rendere "visibile" un'idea... il più dei casi, invece, la foto nasce spontanea, cogliendo l'attimo... Sta lì in stand by, in attesa di un perchè che sorga da un'intuizione, una ricorrenza, un pensiero del momento... E allora mi soccorre la memoria, credo di ricordare quasi tutte le migliaia di foto che ho scattato; l'associazione foto/idea nasce in quel momento, credo sia un attimo di soddisfazione... [https://www.flickr.com/photos/giodoc65] Aspetto di sentire i tuoi commenti a proposito ;) | vedebe: Great street picture | DDuck15: Come sempre sei capace di sorprendermi. title: San Zaccaria, Venice
tags: N/A
description: The Church of San Zaccaria is a 15th-century former monastic church in the sestiere of Castello, central Venice. The present church was built between 1458 and 1515. Antonio Gambello was the original architect, who started the building in the Gothic style, but the upper part of the facade with its arched windows and its columns, and the upper parts of the interior were completed by Mauro Codussi in early Renaissance style seventy years later. The facade is a harmonious Venetian mixture of late-Gothic and Renaissance styles.

title: Campo San Zaccaria, Castello, Venice
tags: church italia venezia
description:
The Church of San Zaccaria is a 15th-century former monastic church in central Venice, Italy.It is dedicated to St. Zechariah, the father of John the Baptist.

The present church was built between 1458 and 1515. Antonio Gambello was the original architect, who started the building in the Gothic style, but the upper part of the facade with its arched windows and its columns, and the upper parts of the interior were completed by Mauro Codussi in early Renaissance style seventy years later. The facade is a harmonious Venetian mixture of late-Gothic and Renaissance styles

title: San Zaccaria. The present church was built in a mixture of Gothic and Renaissance styles between 1458 and 1515. On the left: 16th cen. cloister. Venice 2014

title: San Zaccaria. The first bell tower was demolished in the 11th cen., and rebuilt in the 12th with recycled material from the first with  a pyramid-shaped spire. The spire and belfry collapsed in 1510 and the tower was rebuilt in its current spireless form.

title: FRONTE CHIESA S.ZACCARIA VENEZIA
tags: chiesa fronte szaccaria
comments: aldofurlanetto: La Pala di San Zaccaria è un dipinto ad olio su tavola trasportata su tela (402x273 cm) di Giovanni Bellini, firmato e datato 1505 (IOANNE BELLINVS MCCCCCV, nel cartellino sul gradino del trono), e conservato nella chiesa di San Zaccaria a Venezia. Ridolfi, nel 1648, ricordò la grande pala come commissionata in memoria di Pietro Cappello e come tenuta in grande considerazione quale "una delle più belle e delicate dell'autore[1]". Si tratta della prima opera in cui i debiti verso l'arte di Giorgione sono innegabili e dimostra il rinnovamento intrapreso dall'artista ormai settantenne, che inaugura la sua ultima fase produttiva, quella tonalista[2]. Descrizione e stile[modifica | modifica sorgente] In una nicchia che sembra sfondare la parete sopra l'altare nella navata della chiesa, Bellini ambientò una sacra conversazione secondo uno schema ormai consolidato, con la Madonna col Bambino su un trono, un angelo musicante sul gradino e quattro santi disposti simmetricamente ai due lati: Pietro apostolo, Caterina d'Alessandria, Lucia e Girolamo[2]. Le figure sono intensamente concentrate, ma con pacata naturalezza[3]. Se l'impostazione generale non differisce molto da opere precedenti come la Pala di San Giobbe (come il catino absidale mosaicato), vi si leggono anche profonde novità, come le aperture laterali sul paesaggio, derivate da un'idea di Alvise Vivarini (nella perduta Pala dei Battutii già a Belluno), che infondono una maggiore luminosità alla scena, in grado di ammorbidire le forme, riscaldare l'atmosfera e generare una nuova armonia fatta di piani ampi, macchie cromatiche (nelle vesti) e toni pacatamente contemplativi[2]. Si tratta dell'adesione di Bellini al tonalismo di Giorgione, resa palese, oltre che dai dati stilistici, anche da citazioni testuali, come le barbe sfumate dei due santi e le loro teste reclinate, che somigliano al san Giuseppe nella Sacra Famiglia Benson di Giorgione, o nella santa Caterina che è identica alla Madonna nella stessa opera giorgionesca. Ma l'assimilazione di Bellini delle novità non è passiva, ma adattata con estrema coerenza al proprio stile, senza rinunciare ad esempio al gusto prettamente quattrocentesco dello studio preparatorio, della chiarezza prospettica e della nitidezza[2]. L'uovo che pende sulla testa di Maria rimanda alla creazione, citando forse la Pala di Brera | aldofurlanetto: | aldofurlanetto: | aldofurlanetto: | aldofurlanetto: | aldofurlanetto:

title: Venedig, Chiesa di San Zaccaria, Cappella di San Tarasio, Polyptychon, Ausschnitt (St. Zachary's Church, Tarasio Chapel, polyptych, detail)
tags: venice polyptych venezia venedig polyptychon sestierecastello kirchesanzaccaria stzacharyschurch cappelladisantarasio tarasiokapelle tarasiochapel chiesadisanzaccara lodovicodaforli stefanoveneziano
description:
Der Altaraufsatz und die Holzarbeiten wurden etwa Mitte des 15. Jh. von Lodovico da Forli ausgeführt. 

Im Zentrum des Polyptychons ist ein Bild der Madonna mit dem Kind zu sehen, das von Stefano Plebanus di Sant'Agnese signiert wurde. Die Bilder des hl. Blasius und des hl. Martin von Tours, beide als Bischöfe gewandet , wurden von Stefano Veneziano 1385 geschaffen.  Die Gemälde des hl. Markus und der hl. Elisabeth, Mutter von Johannes dem Täufer, stammen von Antonio Vivarini ( Werkstatt?) und Giovanni d'Alemagna und werden auf 1441 datiert.

Die Bilder der Predella, die Stefano Veneziano zugeschrieben, zeigen  Stationen aus dem Leben des hl. Thomas Becket und im Zentrum den hl. Apollinaris.

tags: world street trip travel venice sea italy tourism church water beautiful architecture photoshop wonderful square la canal nice fantastic nikon perfect italia republic tour superb awesome famous sigma lagoon charm tourist journey stunning excellent romantic gondola lovely incredible 1020 venezia hdr breathtaking rialto adriatic turism d300 turist giudecca worldfamous serenissima photomatix vaporeto brathtaking slod300
description:
Venice, Italy (Venezia in Italian) is one of the most interesting and lovely places in the world. This sanctuary on a lagoon is virtually the same as it was six hundred years ago, which adds to the fascinating character. Venice has decayed since its heyday and is heavily touristed (there are slightly more tourists than residents), but the romantic charm remains.

The Most Serene Republic of Venice dates back to 827, when a Byzantine Duke moved its seat to what is now known as the Rialto, and for the following 970 years, prospered on trade and under the rule of a Roman-style Senate headed by the Doge. Alas in 1797, the city was conquered by Napoleon, a blow from which the city never recovered. The city was soon merged into Austria-Hungary, then ping-ponged back and forth between Austria and a nascent Italy, but Venice is still a monument to the glory days of the Renaissance, and historical culture still throbs powerfully in the old Italians' veins.

title: Chiesa di San Zaccaria
tags: venice italy church architecture gothic renaissance sanzaccaria 0187sanzaccaria
description: San Zaccaria, Venice One of the things that enthralled me about Venice is that I could put the guidebook away, and just wander aimlessly around the back alleys and walkways. (Wandering the canals is a different story). There was a guaranteed surprise around EVERY corner. The city is visually dense, reeks with history, and operates on a human scale. No cars! We spent a few nights in a bed and breakfast a few "blocks" from the Southern Waterfront (Canale di San Marco). Our host insisted on meeting us near where the boat dropped us off (yes, we took a boat from the airport), as there was no way we were going to find our own way to his place. They saying goes that you have not been to Venice until you have been lost in Venice. At least it's an island - it's hard to go too far astray. We nonchalantly passed this church on the way to our rooms. I went back that evening and photographed it during the blue hour. Academia: Mixture of Gothic and Renaissance styles built between 1444 and 1515. Dedicated to the father of John the Baptist, St. Zacharias. Campo San Zaccaria, Venice. Big On Black
comments:
☩ D L Ennis: Beautiful place and image my friend! | Jill Clardy: How wonderful to be there in the evening with the freedom to wander around after the hordes of tourists have departed; I would love to have more time there - we just spent the day which is exhausting and of course you don't get the best light - this is gorgeous ! | kgrin: Worth going back for the late light.  Impressive that you didn't get lost - but getting lost is a bonus, indeed. Good identification! | bats...: Very cool, Alan. | onwatersedge: i like your vantage point, it's a beautiful place and photograph. | 1987porsche944: Wow, gorgeous, and there's no one there! Where are all the people? ^_^ It almost sort of looks creepy, with the very dark windows up there. One of my favorite movie directors of all time is Dario Argento, I think mainly because of his spooky settings in and around Italy. | frank.piaseinza: amazing perspective

ciao
